travel_advisory.title: Exercise Increased Caution
travel_advisory.description: US State Department advises exercising increased caution in Indonesia due to terrorism and natural disasters. Bali itself is generally considered safer than many other parts of Indonesia.

safety_summary: Bali is generally safe for tourists with moderate precautions. Most visitors experience trouble-free trips, though awareness is needed for traffic safety, petty crime, and tourist scams. The island has good tourism infrastructure and low violent crime rates.
key_risks: Petty theft and phone snatching in tourist areas

crime_safety.title: Crime Safety
crime_safety.content: Bali has relatively low crime rates compared to many global destinations. Petty theft accounts for 40% of reported crimes, with incidents mainly involving pickpocketing, bag snatching, and phone theft in crowded areas like Kuta, Seminyak, and Canggu. Violent crime targeting tourists is rare. Most criminal incidents are opportunistic and occur around tourist attractions and transportation hubs. The island has good police presence in tourist areas, with dedicated tourist police stations providing English-language assistance.

scams_fraud.title: Scams and Fraud
scams_fraud.content: Common tourist scams include money changer fraud, overcharging by taxis without meters, fake temple donation requests, and inflated prices for tours. Transport-related scams are frequent, with some taxi drivers claiming broken meters or quoting inflated flat rates. Money exchange scams involve sleight-of-hand tricks or hidden fees at unofficial kiosks. Visitors should use reputable exchange services, insist on taxi meters, and verify prices before agreeing to services.

transportation.title: Transportation Safety
transportation.content: Traffic safety is the biggest risk for tourists in Bali. Scooter accidents are common due to narrow, crowded roads and inexperienced riders. Many tourists underestimate the risks of driving on unfamiliar roads. Ride-hailing apps like Grab and Gojek are generally safer alternatives. Public transportation is limited, making private transport or ride-hailing preferred options. International driving permits are required and strictly enforced. Traffic flows on the left side, and road conditions can be challenging.

health_medical.title: Health and Medical
health_medical.content: Bali has good private healthcare facilities, particularly in tourist areas. Major hospitals like BIMC, Siloam, and Bali International Hospital offer international-standard care with English-speaking staff. Common health risks include 'Bali belly' (food poisoning), dengue fever, and dehydration. Tap water is not safe to drink. Medical evacuation insurance is recommended as serious cases may require transfer to Jakarta or Singapore. Prescription medications should be brought from home as some may be restricted or unavailable.

natural_disasters.title: Natural Disasters
natural_disasters.content: Bali sits on the Pacific Ring of Fire, making it susceptible to earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, and tsunamis. Mount Agung is an active volcano monitored closely by authorities. Recent earthquake activity includes a magnitude 4.8 earthquake near Denpasar in March 2026. Tsunami risk exists but is considered low, with Kuta and Sanur being most vulnerable coastal areas. The government has early warning systems and evacuation procedures in place. Disasters are infrequent but travelers should stay informed through official channels.

women_solo_travel.title: Women's Safety & Solo Travel
women_solo_travel.content: Bali is considered one of the safer Southeast Asian destinations for solo female travelers. Most women report feeling comfortable and welcome, particularly in tourist areas like Ubud, Canggu, and Seminyak. Street harassment is relatively uncommon, and aggressive behavior is rare. The Hindu-majority culture emphasizes respect and karma. Safety concerns are mainly around nightlife areas and when using transport after dark. Many successful solo female travelers visit annually, with good support networks and women-friendly accommodations available.

lgbtq_travel.title: LGBTQ+ Travel
lgbtq_travel.content: LGBTQ+ rights in Indonesia are complex, with homosexuality legal in most areas but same-sex marriage not recognized. New criminal code effective January 2026 criminalizes sex outside marriage, but government officials have stated it won't target tourists or same-sex couples specifically. Bali is more tolerant than other parts of Indonesia, particularly in tourist areas. However, public displays of affection should remain discreet. LGBTQ+ travelers generally report safe experiences in Bali's tourist zones, though caution is advised outside these areas.

nightlife_safety.title: Nightlife Safety
nightlife_safety.content: Bali's nightlife is generally safe when normal precautions are taken. Popular areas include Seminyak, Kuta, and Canggu, each with different atmospheres. Main safety concerns involve alcohol-related incidents, drink spiking (though rare), and transportation after drinking. Indonesia has strict drug laws with severe penalties including imprisonment. Venues in tourist areas have security measures, but travelers should watch their drinks, pace alcohol consumption, and arrange safe transportation home. Private drivers or ride-hailing apps are recommended over driving after drinking.

political_stability.title: Political Stability
political_stability.content: Indonesia faces ongoing political tensions and civil unrest risks, though Bali remains relatively stable due to its tourism importance. The country ranks in the lower third globally for political stability, with recent protests occurring in 2025. However, Bali's economy depends heavily on tourism (80% of GDP), providing strong incentives for maintaining security. Political instability is more common in other Indonesian regions like Papua. Demonstrations are infrequent in Bali's tourist areas, and the government prioritizes visitor safety.

general_safety: Use reputable ride-hailing apps like Grab or Gojek
Carry minimal cash and secure valuables
Keep digital copies of important documents
health_precautions: Drink only bottled or filtered water
Eat at busy restaurants with high turnover
Use insect repellent to prevent dengue
scam_prevention: Use only official money changers with licenses
Insist on taxi meters or use ride-hailing apps
Confirm prices before agreeing to services
Pay only at official temple entrance counters
Be wary of unsolicited offers or help
cultural_sensitivity: Remove shoes before entering sacred areas
Respect local customs and religious ceremonies
Learn basic Indonesian phrases for emergencies
Be mindful of noise levels in residential areas

best_areas_to_stay.description: Cultural center with low crime, pedestrian-friendly, strong community feel
Quiet beach town, paved boardwalk, relaxed atmosphere, family-friendly
Upscale resort area with security, controlled environment, excellent for families
Upmarket beach area with good infrastructure, some nightlife safety considerations
Popular with digital nomads, busy traffic, moderate safety with awareness

dry_season.safety_notes: Better weather conditions, more tourist activity, higher accommodation demand

wet_season.safety_notes: Increased risk of flooding, dengue outbreaks possible, some outdoor venues may reduce hours

solo_travelers.advice: Generally safe with good backpacker infrastructure. Stay in well-reviewed accommodations, inform someone of your plans, avoid isolated areas at night.

families.advice: Very family-friendly destination. Main concerns are traffic safety and pool security. Choose resorts with proper safety measures and supervise children near roads.

elderly_travelers.advice: Good healthcare available in tourist areas. Consider mobility limitations on uneven surfaces, heat sensitivity, and ensure comprehensive health insurance.

budget_travelers.advice: Many affordable safe options available. Focus on location over luxury, use public transport alternatives, be extra vigilant with valuables.

recent_incidents.description: Magnitude 4.8 earthquake near Denpasar with no major damage reported
34 men arrested in Surabaya in police operation, highlighting enforcement of moral laws
